coredata.dc:title: Effects of carbon dots in combination with rosemary-inspired carnosic acid on oxidative stability of deep frying oils
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Linlin
Zhang, Min
Wang, Haixiang
Devahastin, Sakamon
coredata.dc:description: Antioxidative carbon dots (CDs) were prepared and first characterized in terms of their morphology, size and microstructure. The feasibility of CDs to enhance oxidative stability of a blend soybean and palm frying oil was then evaluated; the efficacy of CDs was evaluated in comparison with those of rosemary-inspired carnosic acid (CA), combined CA/CDs and tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ). CDs appeared as spherical nanoparticles with the size smaller in general than 1 nm. Their characteristic features could be attributed to their oxygen functional groups and highly defected graphitic structure. CDs exhibited superior properties for scavenging DPPH, hydroxyl and superoxide anion radicals. Both CA and CDs showed significant inhibitory effect against oxidative rancidity of the oil. Interestingly, 0.05% CA/CDs displayed similar inhibitory effect as 0.02% TBHQ in terms of p-anisidine value, total polar compounds content, fatty acids composition, conjugated diene and triene contents as well as color and viscosity of the oil.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antioxidant activity
Free radicals
Molecular structure
Nanoparticles
Oil oxidation
Physicochemical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of antioxidant activities of CDs
Determination of conjugated diene and triene contents
Conjugated diene and triene contents of oils

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs, (b) Particle size distribution of CDs, (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs, and (d) Raman spectrum of CDs.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs, (b) PL excitation and emission spectra of CDs, (C) PL emission spectra of CDs at various excitation wavelengths.

(a) Absorption spectra of DPPH solutions at various CDs concentrations, (b) Scavenging effect of CDs on DPPH radicals (inset: color changes of DPPH solutions with CD content), (c) Absorbance spectra of FeSO4-salicylic acid-H2O2 solutions at various CD concentrations, (d) Scavenging effect of CDs on •OH radicals (inset: color changes of FeSO4-salicylic acid-H2O2 solutions with CD content), (e) Absorbance spectra of xanthine-NBT-xanthine oxidase solutions at various CD concentrations, (f) Scavenging effect of CDs on O2 •− radicals (inset: color changes of xanthine-NBT-xanthine oxidase solutions with CDs content). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Changes in (a) AV, (b) PV, (c) p-AnV, and (d) TPC of frying oils with different antioxidants during 35 frying batches.
Accumulated changes of fatty acids after 35 frying batches.
(a) Change of K232, and (b) Change of K268 in oil samples with different antioxidants during 35 frying batches.
Viscosity changes of frying oils treated with various antioxidants during 35 frying batches.
Color changes of frying oils treated with different antioxidants.

head.ce:title: Effects of carbon dots in combination with rosemary-inspired carnosic acid on oxidative stability of deep frying oils

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, 214122, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antioxidative carbon dots (CDs) were prepared and first characterized in terms of their morphology, size and microstructure. The feasibility of CDs to enhance oxidative stability of a blend soybean and palm frying oil was then evaluated; the efficacy of CDs was evaluated in comparison with those of rosemary-inspired carnosic acid (CA), combined CA/CDs and tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ). CDs appeared as spherical nanoparticles with the size smaller in general than 1 nm. Their characteristic features could be attributed to their oxygen functional groups and highly defected graphitic structure. CDs exhibited superior properties for scavenging DPPH, hydroxyl and superoxide anion radicals. Both CA and CDs showed significant inhibitory effect against oxidative rancidity of the oil. Interestingly, 0.05% CA/CDs displayed similar inhibitory effect as 0.02% TBHQ in terms of p-anisidine value, total polar compounds content, fatty acids composition, conjugated diene and triene contents as well as color and viscosity of the oil.

ce:section.ce:para: Deep frying using oils as heating medium is a culinary process commonly applied in household and industrial production (Fan, Zhang, Xiao, Sun, & Tao, 2005; Song, Zhang, & Mujumdar, 2007). During deep frying, oil is known to be under the influences of fatty acids composition, water activity, oxygen content, temperature and some metal ions; these factors contribute to oxidative degradation of the oil, which involves decomposition of neutral lipids into glycerol and fatty acids (bib_Aladedunye_and_Matthäus_2014 Aladedunye & Matthäus, 2014). Unsaturated fatty acids would then degrade into aldehydes and ketones; these small molecules produce off odors and reduce the oil quality. In addition, aldehydes and ketones can participate in the Maillard reaction due to their reactive carbonyl groups, resulting in the formation of health hazardous substances, including acrylamide, heterocyclic amines, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural and advanced glycation end-products (Zamora & Hidalgo, 2005).

In order to prolong the service life of oil, antioxidants are typically added. Antioxidants can be divided into synthetic antioxidants, which include butylated hydroxyanisole (BHA), butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT) and tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) (Chirinos, Huaman, Betalleluz-Pallardel, Pedreschi, & Campos, 2011), and natural antioxidants such as phenols, natural pigments, phytic acid, peptides and amino acids (Wang, Dong, Wang, Wang, & Fan, 2020), which extracted from natural plants and animals (Huang & Zhang, 2012; Tao, Zhang, & Yu, 2007; Wu, Zhang, Wang, Mothibe, & Chen, 2012; Zhang et al., 2017). Studies have nevertheless revealed that although synthetic antioxidants have the characteristics of low price, high temperature resistance and exhibit higher antioxidant activity, they have certain toxicity or potential carcinogenicity as well as weak estrogen-like effects (Hwang, Winkler-Moser, & Liu, 2012). A certain types of synthetic antioxidants have indeed been banned or marked as to be used with caution in the US, Europe and Japan, among other countries. Study of various alternative synthetic antioxidants is therefore of increasing interest.

Carbon dots (CDs) refer to a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with the size of smaller than 10 nm and are generally composed of hydrogen, oxygen and carbon (Li et al., 2018). Hydrothermal carbonization has extensively been employed to fabricate CDs; compared with other methods, hydrothermal carbonization has the advantages of simplicity, economy, high efficiency and eco-friendliness. Carbon sources that can be used for such a synthesis method are also widely available, non-toxic and inexpensive, for they are mostly natural raw materials (Chunduri et al., 2016). Orange juice, lemon juice, grape juice, oats, plant leaves, potato, milk and even waste biomass can be used as precursors for the fabrication of CDs (Sachdev & Gopinath, 2015).

As for the application of CDs in food processing and preservation, due to their fluorescence quenching effect, many studies have been devoted to the use of CDs for detecting foreign compounds and pathogenic bacteria in foods. Antibacterial activity of CDs has also widely been recognized. Studies have indeed been conducted on the use of CDs to inactivate Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Bacillus subtilis, molds and yeasts to maintain the storage quality of fresh-cut cucumber (Fan, Zhang, Fan, & Jiang, 2019) and to prolong the shelf-life of soymilk (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020) and packaged soy products (Zhao, Zhang, Bhandari, & Bai, 2020). Antioxidant activity of CDs has also been explored. CDs fabricated by hydrothermal means using different carbon sources such as citric acid, garlic and date molasses have shown to exhibit reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging activity in vitro; in some cases antioxidant effects of CDs are comparable to those of standard synthetic antioxidants (Ruiz, Yate, Garcia, Cabanero, & Grande, 2017). Studies on the antioxidant activity of CDs are nevertheless still limited; most works also focused only on scavenging of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radicals and hydroxyl free radicals. It is therefore of interest and in fact importance to explore alternative uses of CDs with antioxidant activity in various food processing and preservation situations. Combined use of CDs with other natural compounds to create synergistic antioxidant effect is also of much interest but still lacking.

Rosemary extract is among the popular natural antioxidants that have widely been applied in a variety of foods, including meat products, snacks, roasted nuts, fish oils and even frying oils (Wu et al., 2019). Such an extract exhibits higher contents of phenolic compounds, which can help inhibit lipid photooxidation and autoxidation, and reduce oxidation products/markers, including thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS), hexanal, peroxide value (PV) and p-anisidine value (p-AnV) (Urbančič, Kolar, Dimitrijevic, Demsar, & Vidrih, 2014). Rosemary extract has also been reported to possess a positive effect on reducing the degree of rancidity and preventing color changes of foods (Wu et al., 2019). Rosemary extract contains sixteen phenolic compounds, of which rosmarinic acid (RA), carnosic acid (CA) and carnosol (CN) are the major active components. CA is nevertheless considered the most effective antioxidant in rosemary extract due to its super-stoichiometric characteristics; CA also exhibits superior oil solubility (Masuda, Inaba, & Takeda, 2001) and hence is suitable for use to maintain the quality of frying oils. Synergistic effect of this extract and CDs, despite their potential, on stability and quality of frying oils has never been investigated, however.

In this study, selected characteristics (morphology, size and molecular structure) and antioxidant activities of CDs from banana were first investigated. Banana is one of the common fruit in the world and is a good and green source of carbon due to its high carbohydrate content, wide range of sources, and low cost (Jiang, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Lim, 2014). Use of CDs, CA and CA/CDs blends to enhance oxidative stability and apparent quality of a frying oil was then explored. The results were compared with that of the control oil sample with no antioxidant and that of the sample containing TBHQ as an antioxidant.

Banana was bought from a local grocery shop in Wuxi, China. Soybean oil, palm oil and meatballs were provided by Yechun Food Co., Ltd. (Yangzhou, China). All other reagents were of chromatographic or analytical grade and were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) or Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

CDs were prepared as per the suggested methods of Zhao, Zhang, Wang, and Devahastin (2020). Briefly, 30 g of banana was first blended into puree with 80 mL of deionized water. Forty mL of the puree and 40 mL of ethanol were then transferred to a 100-mL hydrothermal reaction vessel, which was subsequently put in a muffle furnace at 150 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the resulting product was centrifuged at 3000×g. Supernatant was collected and filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm). The filtrate was dialyzed in a 500-Da dialysis bag for 24 h. CDs powder was finally obtained by freeze drying of the dialysate.

The morphology of CDs was observed via the use of a transmission electron microscope (JEOL, JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan) at 200 kV. Particle size of the CDs was determined using a particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Worcestershire, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the CDs was obtained using a spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Nicolet, iS10, Waltham, MA, USA). Raman spectrum of the CDs was determined using a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA Jobin Yvon S.A.S, France). Ultraviolet/visible absorbances of the CDs were acquired using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 35, Waltham, MA, USA), while fluorescence spectra of the CDs was obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000, Fukuoka, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidant activities of CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
The scavenging capacity of CDs against DPPH radicals was estimated as per the methods of Sachdev and Gopinath (2015) with some modification. In brief, 1 mL of ethanolic DPPH solution (0.1 mM) was mixed with 1 mL of CDs at various concentrations (0.5–64 μg/mL). After the reaction in dark for 20 min, the absorbance was recorded at 517 nm. Percentage of DPPH scavenging capacity of CDs was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
% DPPH scavenging capacity =
(
1 -
A i
/
A 0
)
× 100 %
where A i and A 0 represent the absorbance of the DPPH solution with and without CDs, respectively. Vitamin C was used as the standard antioxidant.

The ability of CDs to scavenge hydroxyl free radicals (•OH) was determined via the Fenton reaction (Du et al., 2020). Briefly, salicylic acid ethanol solution (1.8 mM, 600 μL) and FeSO4 solution (7.5 mM, 200 μL) were mixed with CDs (0.03–4 mg/mL, 100 μL); this was followed by the addition of H2O2 (200 mM, 200 μL). Finally, 900 μL of doubly distilled water was added into the mixture. After incubation at 37 °C for 15 min, the absorbance was recorded at 510 nm. The scavenging capacity was estimated using Eq. (2):
(2)
% • OH scavenging capacity =
(
1 -
A j
/
A 0
)
× 100 %
where A j and A 0 represent the absorbance of the mixtures with and without CDs, respectively. Vitamin C was used as the standard antioxidant.

The ability of CDs to scavenge superoxide anion free radicals (O2 •−) was determined as per the methods of Bae and Suh (2007) with some modification. Briefly, 500 μL of xanthine (0.4 mM) was mixed with 500 μL of 0.24 mM nitro blue tetrazolium chloride; this was followed by mixing with 1 mL of xanthine oxidase (0.049 unit/mL). Then, 500 μL of CDs at different concentrations (0.016–2 mg/mL) was added. After 30 min of the reaction at 37 °C, the absorbance was recorded at 550 nm. The percentage of O2 •− scavenging capacity of CDs was calculated as per Eq. (3):
(3)
%
O 2
• -
scavenging activity =
(
1 -
A k
/
A 0
)
× 100 %
where A k and A 0 represent the absorbance of the mixtures with and without CDs, respectively. Vitamin C was again used as the standard antioxidant.

A homogeneous blend (3:1, v/v) of soybean oil and palm oil was used as the test oil in this study. Four types of antioxidants were added to an oil sample, including TBHQ (0.02%), CA (0.05%), CDs (0.05%), and CA/CDs (0.025% CA + 0.025% CDs); the control sample was the one without any added antioxidant.

Two meatballs (60 g each) were fried in each oil sample; frying was performed in a 1.5-L stainless steel fryer (WeighMax, XJ-6K116, Huanggang, China). The fryer was filled with 1 L of each oil sample; the oil was heated to 170 °C and kept at 170 ± 5 °C during each frying batch (8 min). A total of 35 frying batches were conducted for each oil sample. At the end of each 5 frying batches, 50 mL of an oil sample was sampled and kept at −20 °C until further analysis.

Acid value (AV), peroxide value (PV), p-anisidine value (p-AnV) and total polar compounds (TPC) content of an oil sample were determined as per the National Standards of PR China GB 5009.229–2016, GB 5009.227–2016, GB/T 24304–2009, and GB 5009.202–2016, respectively.

Fatty acids profile of each oil sample was determined as fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) using a gas chromatograph (Shimadzu, GC-2010 PLUS, Kyoto, Japan) fitted with DB-WAX capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm) and flame ionization detector (FID). Fatty acids were methylated by the boron trifluoride assay as per the National Standards of PR China GB 5009.168–2016.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of conjugated diene and triene contents
ce:section.ce:para: Conjugated diene (K232) and conjugated triene (K268) contents were determined as Chinese National Standard GB/T 22500–2008. An oil sample was dissolved in isooctane and the absorbance was recorded at 232 nm and 268 nm.

Color of an oil sample was measured using a chroma meter (Konica Minolta, CR-400, Tokyo, Japan). The results are given in terms of CIE L*, a* and b*, where L* refers to lightness, a* refers to redness/greenness and b* refers to yellowness/blueness.

A rotating-plate viscometer (Lichen, NDJ-8S, Shaoxing, China) fitted with spindle No. 1 was used for the viscosity determination. A fixed volume (200 mL) of an oil sample was allowed to rotate at 60 rpm during the measurement.

Experiments were completely randomized in design and repeated three times. Measurements of each oil sample were also repeated three times. All data were subject to one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and are shown as mean values with standard deviations. Statistical differences among the mean values were identified using Tukey's test at a confidence level of 95%. SPSS (version 21.0; SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used for all the statistical calculations.

Fig. 1 a shows the transmission electron microscopic image of CDs. The image demonstrates that CDs prepared from banana appeared as spherical nanoparticles with good monodispersity. The particle size distribution data shown in Fig. 1b reveal that the diameters of CDs were distributed between 0.54 and 1.5 nm, but were mostly smaller than 1 nm.

Molecular structure of CDs was first investigated via FTIR spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 1c. The strong absorbance at 3394 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds, while the characteristic peak at 2928 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibration. The absorbances at 1701 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching bending vibrations of CO and CC, while the peak at 1028 cm−1 is attributed to the C–O stretching vibration. The above results indicate that CDs were functionalized with oxygen functional groups, which may include hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. Raman spectroscopy was also conducted and the results are shown in Fig. 1d. Raman spectrum revealed two distinct peaks, the D peak and the G peak at 1348 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively; these peaks represent disordered carbon structure and ordered graphitic structure, respectively. The prominent D and G peaks confirmed that the prepared CDs had highly defected graphitic structure (Zhu et al., 2019). It is indeed these defect structures that give the carbon material the characteristics of photoluminescence.

UV absorption spectrum of CDs exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 283 nm (Fig. 2 a). This was believed to be induced by the π-π* electronic transition in the conjugated CC band and n-π* transition of the CO band (De & Karak, 2013), which demonstrates that the CDs structure had aromatic sp2 C domains (Zhu et al., 2019). The fluorescence spectra reveal that the optimal excitation wavelength was located at 370 nm, while the emission wavelength was located at 460 nm (Fig. 2b). As mentioned by De and Karak (2013), the intensity and position of the maximum emission depend on the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength changes, the emission peak shifts; the intensity also changes. As can be seen in Fig. 2c, when the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 500 nm, the maximum emission shifted, while the intensity first increased and then gradually decreased.

Antioxidant activity is here reported in terms of EC50 value, which is the concentration of an antioxidant corresponding to the free radicals scavenging rate of 50%. EC50 values of vitamin C, which was used for comparison purposes, toward DPPH radicals, •OH radicals and O2 •− radicals were noted to be 6.09 μg/mL, 0.21 mg/mL and 62.93 μg/mL, respectively.

It is seen from the results shown in Fig. 3 a that when the concentration of CDs increased, the absorption intensity of DPPH radicals at 517 nm decreased, indicating that the scavenging capacity of CDs for DPPH radicals was in a dose-dependent manner. With the CDs concentration raised from 0.5 to 64 μg/mL, the scavenging activity increased from 2.41% to 93.82%; the color of ethanolic DPPH solution changed from deep purple to yellow (Fig. 3b) due to the generation of stable DPPH-H molecules. EC50 value was noted to be 15.02 μg/mL, which is slightly higher than that of vitamin C (6.09 μg/mL). Similar results were reported by Sachdev and Gopinath (2015); EC50 of CDs prepared from coriander leaves against DPPH radicals was noted by those investigators to be 15 μg/mL. Such a superior DPPH scavenging capacity is most probably due to the large number of functional groups (mostly hydroxyl, which is a hydrogen donor group) present on the surfaces of CDs (Chunduri et al., 2016). When DPPH radicals were in contact with hydrogen, generation of DPPH-H (a stable complex) took place, turning then the solution color into colorless or pale yellow (Shen, Shang, Chen, Wang, & Cai, 2017).

In terms of the scavenging capacity toward •OH radicals, similar to the case of DPPH radicals, as the concentration of CDs increased, the scavenging rate increased (Fig. 3c). The color of the solution turned from deep purple to yellow. Based on the data shown in Fig. 3d, EC50 value of CDs against hydroxyl radicals was calculated to be 0.94 mg/mL. Our CDs exhibited higher scavenging activity toward •OH radicals than those separated from Maillard reaction products (EC50 = 12.23 mg/mL) as reported by Li et al. (2018). The mechanism responsible for •OH scavenging of CDs may involve hydrogen donation, electron transfer or sp2 C sites (Ruiz, Yate, García, Cabanero, & Grande, 2017). Qiu et al. (2014) indeed reported that the sp2 C domain was the dominant •OH scavenging sites instead of the oxygen functional groups.

In terms of the scavenging capacity toward O2 •− radicals, which are the most abundant free radicals in human body and can induce lipid peroxidation, accelerate aging and cause cancers as well as skin and cardiovascular diseases (Sunil, Duraipandiyan, Ignacimuthu, & Al-Dhabi, 2013), the results shown in Fig. 3e and f reveal that our CDs exhibited excellent ability to scavenge O2 •− radicals. EC50 value of CDs against O2 •− radicals was 96.14 μg/mL, slightly higher than that of vitamin C (62.93 μg/mL). All the above results demonstrate that our CDs exhibit antioxidant activities against various free radicals.

Under a high-temperature frying condition, triglycerides would first degrade into free fatty acids (FFA) through hydrolysis reactions and thermal oxidation, leading to an increase in AV of frying oil. AV can therefore be used to reflect FFA content and hence oxidative rancidity of the oil (Song et al., 2017). The influences of different antioxidants on AV of frying oils are shown in Fig. 4 a. During the whole frying process, AV of all oil samples significantly increased. The increase was most obvious in the case of the control sample, from 0.14 mg KOH/g before frying to 1.19 KOH mg/g, which was significantly higher (p < 0.05) than the values of antioxidant groups. This was followed by the oil sample with added CA; the final AV was 0.79 mg KOH/g. The oil sample added with CDs experienced the slightest increase in AV, with the final value of only 0.61 KOH mg/g, which was significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those of other groups. The effects of all the antioxidants on delaying an increase in AV could be ranked as follows: 0.05% CDs >0.05% CA/CDs >0.02% TBHQ >0.05% CA.

Peroxide is a primary product of lipid oxidation and its formation and decomposition during frying is a dynamic equilibrium process. During an initial period of frying, the peroxide generation rate is higher than the degradation rate, hence an increase in PV (Sun, Zhang, & Fan, 2019). It can be seen from Fig. 4b that during an initial period of frying, PV of all five sample groups increased with the increasing number of frying batch. PV subsequently showed a downward trend. This is because peroxide itself is unstable and can easily decompose into other compounds, including aldehydes and ketones. More importantly, it can form toxic secondary oxides with other oil molecules (Blasi et al., 2018). Therefore, a decrease in PV does not imply an improvement in the oil quality. In comparison with the control sample, addition of TBHQ, CA, CDs and CA/CDs could significantly (p < 0.05) delay an increase in PV. After frying for 35 batches, PV of the oil added with CA was the lowest, only 3.27 mmol/kg, which was significantly lower (p < 0.05) than that of the control sample (6.46 mmol/kg). The effects of all the antioxidants on delaying an increase in PV could be ranked as follows: 0.05% CA > 0.05% CA/CDs >0.02% TBHQ >0.05% CDs.

p-AnV refers to the content of aldehyde compounds degraded by oil and fat, which are not easy to decompose at higher temperatures (Heng, Ong, & Chow, 2020). Therefore, p-AnV would be more suitable to be used to estimate the degree of oil oxidation than PV. It can be seen from Fig. 4c that p-AnV of the fresh control oil sample was only 1.07, while it increased to 65.74 after frying for 35 batches. Adding antioxidants could effectively delay an increase in p-AnV. The delaying effects of CA/CDs and TBHQ were the strongest; p-AnV only increased by 27.00 and 27.82, which were significantly lower (p < 0.05) than the values observed when CA (35.16) and CDs (31.18) were used. It is worth noting that there was no significant difference (p > 0.05) in p-AnV when using 0.02% TBHQ and 0.05% CA/CDs to treat the oils.

Measurement of total polar compounds content can help evaluate deterioration of frying oil; the method has been applied to judge the quality of frying oil or cooking oil in many countries (Adjonu, Zhou, Prenzler, Ayton, & Blanchard, 2019). Fig. 4d shows that the control oil sample contained the highest TPC content during the whole frying period. This indicates that CA, CDs, CA/CDs and TBHQ all exhibited significant antioxidative effects. From 15th to 35th frying batches, TPC contents of the oils treated with TBHQ and CA/CDs were significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those of the oils treated with CA and CDs, indicating that the performance of TBHQ and CA/CDs was significantly higher (p < 0.05) than that of CA and CDs. Similar to the results on p-AnV, TPC contents of the oils treated with 0.02% TBHQ and 0.05% CA/CDs showed no significant difference (p > 0.05).

The above results can be explained by the fact that there are two hydroxyl groups on the benzene ring of CA. Hydroxyl groups are known to be a good hydrogen donor and can help eliminate singlet oxygen, hydroxyl as well as lipid peroxyl radicals and hence effectively prevent lipid peroxidation (Zhang, Chen, Yang, & Zu, 2010). The antioxidative mechanism of CDs is nevertheless still unclear, but it is certain that the large amount of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs is one of the reasons for their superior antioxidant activity. Similar to CA, CDs can then help prevent lipid peroxidation of oils during deep frying.

Based on the AV and PV results, 0.05% CDs exhibited superior ability to delay an increase in AV, while 0.05% CA possessed higher ability to delay an increase in PV. The antioxidative effect of 0.05% CA/CDs was stronger than those of CDs or CA alone. Such superiority was reflected through the results on p-AnV and TPC although the mechanism of interaction between the two antioxidants is still not yet fully understood.

By evaluating the changes in the composition of fatty acids, thermal oxidation of oils during deep frying can be assessed (Fig. 5 ). There was a small amount of minor fatty acids that did not change upon frying, which may be because of their lower contents in the oils (<3%); these fatty acids are not therefore shown in the figure. By observing the variations of the major fatty acids, it was noted that after 35 batches of frying, the contents of linolenic acid (C18:3) and linoleic acid (C18:2) decreased, while the contents of oleic acid (C18:1), stearic acid (C18:0) and palmitic acid (C16:0) increased. This is because the double bonds of some unsaturated fatty acids (UFA) were broken during the frying process. Such acids were then converted into saturated fatty acids (SFA) and hence the increased contents of SFA. C18:1, C18:0 and C16:0 fractions in the control oil sample experienced the largest increase. The increase in the oil with added TBHQ was the smallest; this was followed by the increase in the oil with added CA/CDs. On the other hand, C18:2 and C18:3 fractions in the oil added with CA/CDs experienced the smallest decrease; this was followed by the oil with added TBHQ.

The ratio of C18:2 to C16:0 (C18:2/C16:0) was taken as an indicator to monitor the change in the fatty acids composition (Aniołowska & Kita, 2016). The values belonging to the oils with added CA/CDs and TBHQ decreased by 0.48 and 0.49; the decreases were smaller than those observed in the other three groups of oil samples. From this point of view, it can be said that the antioxidant activity of 0.05% CA/CDs is comparable to that of 0.02% TBHQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugated diene and triene contents of oils
ce:section.ce:para: Changes in the absorbance of oil at 232 nm and 268 nm are used as an efficient parameter to estimate the degree of oxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA). K232 indicates the existence of conjugated dienes produced by PUFA degradation, while K268 is attributed to the generation of secondary oxidation products, including carbonyl compounds and conjugated trienes (Wang et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b, K232 and K268 contents of the five oil groups increased with the increased number of frying batch; the increase in the case of the control sample was significantly higher (p < 0.05) than those of the other samples. After frying for 35 batches, K232 and K268 contents of the oil added with TBHQ were the lowest. This was followed by the oil added with CA/CDs; no significant differences (p > 0.05) in the contents were noted among the two sample groups, however; and the values of the two groups were significantly lower (p < 0.05) than those of CA group and CDs group. These result further demonstrate that 0.05% CA/CDs can achieve similar antioxidative effect to 0.02% TBHQ.

Formation and decomposition of oxidation products, including hydroxides, ketones, conjugated dienoic acids and hydroperoxides result in the oil color change from light yellow to dark brown (Asadi & Farahmandfar, 2020). In addition, protein components of a material being fried may penetrate into the oil and react with carbonyl compounds generated by lipid oxidation during the frying process; such reactions would then darken the oil color (Sun et al., 2019).

According to visual observation, the color of all oil samples increasingly darkened with the number of frying batch. As listed in Table 1 , L* and b* values of all the samples decreased with extended frying; a* value, on the other hand, expectedly increased. It should be noted that since CA and CDs were brown-black in color, mixing either of them into oil would darken the color of the oil and might have affected the judgment of oil color. Nevertheless, it is still seen from the results in Table 1 that L* and b* values of the control sample were significantly lower than those of the other samples. This indicates that the added antioxidants could all effectively inhibit color changes of the oils upon frying.

Viscosity is another important physical property of frying oil; its value is indeed related to the oil quality, oil type and temperature. As frying is extended, a large amount of macromolecules would form, resulting then in an increase in the degree of polymerization and hence an increase in the oil viscosity (Asokapandian, Swamy, & Hajjul, 2019). It can be seen in Fig. 7 that control sample showed significant increase (p < 0.05) in viscosity when compared with antioxidant groups. After frying for 35 batches, the viscosity of the oil added with TBHQ increased the least. There was no significant difference (p > 0.05) in the viscosity values of the samples added with TBHQ and CA/CDs, suggesting that CA/CDs can be used as a substitute for TBHQ to improve oxidative stability of frying oil.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from banana and were first characterized before being used to enhance oxidative stability of a frying oil (blend of soybean and palm oils). The prepared CDs appeared as spherical nanoparticles with good monodispersity, with the sizes smaller in general than 1 nm. The characteristic features of the CDs could be attributed to oxygen functional groups (O–H, CO and C–O), which were identified via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy at wavenumbers of 3394 cm−1, 1701 cm−1 and 1028 cm−1. The D and G peaks in the Raman spectrum reveal that the CDs had highly defected graphitic structure, which is the reason for their photoluminescence. Their abilities to scavenge DPPH radicals, •OH radicals and O2 •− radicals were also confirmed. Similar to rosemary-inspired carnosic acid (CA), the CDs showed desirable capability of inhibiting oxidative rancidity of the frying oil, especially when viewing from the acid value of the oil. Interestingly, 0.05% CA/CDs blend was noted to possess similar inhibitory effect as 0.02% TBHQ in terms of p-anisidine value, total polar compounds content, fatty acids composition, conjugated diene and triene contents as well as color and viscosity of the oil. The prepared CDs therefore have a potential to be used as an alternative antioxidant, either alone or in combination with other natural antioxidant, in lieu of a synthetic antioxidant to stabilize an oil used in the frying process.

Literature research and experiments were performed by Linlin Zhao. Oversight of the project and field experience were provided by Min Zhang, Haixiang Wang and Sakamon Devahastin. Critical review of the manuscript was performed by Min Zhang, Haixiang Wang and Sakamon Devahastin.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial supports from the China Key Research Program (Contract No. 2017YFD0400501), Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R&D Program (No.YZ2019034), the 111 Project (BP0719028), Research Innovation Project for Postgraduates in Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX20_1846), Jiangsu Province (China) “Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control” Industry Development Program, all of which enabled us to carry out this study.
